title: Trust, Safety, and Accuracy: Assessing LLMs for Routine Maternity Advice
summary: Access to reliable maternal healthcare information is a major challenge in rural India due to limited medical resources and infrastructure. With over 830 million internet users and nearly half of rural women online, digital tools offer new opportunities for health education. This study evaluates large language models (LLMs) like ChatGPT-4o, Perplexity AI, and GeminiAI to provide reliable and understandable pregnancy-related information. Seventeen pregnancy-focused questions were posed to each model and compared with responses from maternal health professionals. Evaluations used semantic similarity, noun overlap, and readability metrics to measure content quality. Results show Perplexity closely matched expert semantics, while ChatGPT-4o produced clearer, more understandable text with better medical terminology. As internet access grows in rural areas, LLMs could serve as scalable aids for maternal health education. The study highlights the need for AI tools that balance accuracy and clarity to improve healthcare communication in underserved regions.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well large language models can provide reliable, easy-to-understand pregnancy information compared to human maternal health experts in rural India.
AI.motivation: Rural India faces significant barriers to accessing reliable maternal healthcare information due to shortages of medical staff and infrastructure. At the same time, internet penetration and smartphone use among rural women are rapidly increasing, creating an opportunity to use digital tools for health education. However, it is unclear whether LLMs can deliver pregnancy-related information that is both accurate and understandable for this population, which motivates a systematic evaluation.
AI.method: The authors selected 17 questions focused on pregnancy and posed each question to three LLMs: ChatGPT-4o, Perplexity AI, and GeminiAI. They collected responses from each model and from maternal health professionals, treating the expert responses as a reference. They then evaluated the model outputs using quantitative metrics: semantic similarity to expert answers, noun overlap to assess content coverage, and readability indicators to gauge clarity and comprehensibility of the text, particularly in terms of medical terminology and general readability.
AI.result: Perplexity AI produced answers whose semantic content most closely matched that of the maternal health experts, suggesting higher alignment in factual content. ChatGPT-4o, while slightly less similar semantically, generated responses that were clearer, more readable, and used medical terminology in a way that was easier for lay users to understand. GeminiAI performed less strongly relative to these two models on the reported metrics.
AI.conclusion: LLMs have promising potential as scalable tools to support maternal health education in rural India, where human medical resources are scarce but internet access is growing. The findings emphasize that an effective AI assistant for healthcare must balance semantic accuracy (being close to expert advice) with clarity and readability for non-expert users. The authors advocate for further development and deployment of AI tools that optimize this balance to improve health communication and education in underserved regions.

title: Enhancing Financial Report Question-Answering: A Retrieval-Augmented Generation System with Reranking Analysis
summary: Financial analysts face significant challenges extracting information from lengthy 10-K reports, which often exceed 100 pages. This paper presents a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system designed to answer questions about S&P 500 financial reports and evaluates the impact of neural reranking on system performance. Our pipeline employs hybrid search combining full-text and semantic retrieval, followed by an optional reranking stage using a cross-encoder model. We conduct systematic evaluation using the FinDER benchmark dataset, comprising 1,500 queries across five experimental groups. Results demonstrate that reranking significantly improves answer quality, achieving 49.0 percent correctness for scores of 8 or above compared to 33.5 percent without reranking, representing a 15.5 percentage point improvement. Additionally, the error rate for completely incorrect answers decreases from 35.3 percent to 22.5 percent. Our findings emphasize the critical role of reranking in financial RAG systems and demonstrate performance improvements over baseline methods through modern language models and refined retrieval strategies.

AI.tldr: The paper builds a RAG system for 10-K question answering and shows that adding a neural reranker substantially improves answer correctness and reduces completely wrong answers.
AI.motivation: Financial analysts struggle to manually extract specific information from very long 10-K filings. Existing QA and RAG systems for finance may not be optimized for these lengthy, detailed documents or may lack rigorous evaluation of retrieval and reranking components. The authors want to make it easier and more accurate to answer questions about S&P 500 firms’ financial reports, and to quantify how much neural reranking helps.
AI.method: They design a Retrieval-Augmented Generation pipeline for answering questions over S&P 500 10-K reports. Retrieval uses a hybrid approach that combines full-text (lexical) and semantic search. Retrieved passages can then be optionally reranked using a cross-encoder model as a second stage. They evaluate this pipeline on the FinDER benchmark, which has 1,500 questions organized into five experimental groups, comparing performance with and without the reranking stage. Answer quality is scored on a scale where 8+ indicates high correctness, and they also track the rate of completely incorrect answers.
AI.result: With neural reranking enabled, the system achieves 49.0% of answers scoring 8 or higher, versus 33.5% without reranking, a 15.5 percentage point gain. The proportion of completely wrong answers drops from 35.3% without reranking to 22.5% with reranking. These results show a substantial positive impact of the reranking component on answer quality and error reduction in the financial RAG setting.
AI.conclusion: Neural reranking is a critical component for high-performing financial RAG systems on long, complex documents like 10-Ks. By combining hybrid retrieval with a cross-encoder reranker and modern language models, the proposed pipeline significantly outperforms baseline setups without reranking. The study underscores that careful retrieval and reranking design is essential to improving accuracy and reliability for question answering over financial reports.

title: Rubric-Guided Fine-tuning of SpeechLLMs for Multi-Aspect, Multi-Rater L2 Reading-Speech Assessment
comment: Accepted to LREC 2026. This publication is part of the project Responsible AI for Voice Diagnostics (RAIVD) with file number NGF.1607.22.013 of the research programme NGF AiNed Fellowship Grants, which is financed by the Dutch Research Council (NWO)
summary: Reliable and interpretable automated assessment of second-language (L2) speech remains a central challenge, as large speech-language models (SpeechLLMs) often struggle to align with the nuanced variability of human raters. To address this, we introduce a rubric-guided reasoning framework that explicitly encodes multi-aspect human assessment criteria: accuracy, fluency, and prosody, while calibrating model uncertainty to capture natural rating variability. We fine-tune the Qwen2-Audio-7B-Instruct model using multi-rater human judgments and develop an uncertainty-calibrated regression approach supported by conformal calibration for interpretable confidence intervals. Our Gaussian uncertainty modeling and conformal calibration approach achieves the strongest alignment with human ratings, outperforming regression and classification baselines. The model reliably assesses fluency and prosody while highlighting the inherent difficulty of assessing accuracy. Together, these results demonstrate that rubric-guided, uncertainty-calibrated reasoning offers a principled path toward trustworthy and explainable SpeechLLM-based speech assessment.

AI.tldr: They build a rubric-guided, uncertainty-aware speech assessment model that better matches human ratings of L2 speech.
AI.motivation: Automated scoring of second-language speech is important but difficult because human raters are nuanced and variable, and existing large SpeechLLMs don’t align well with this variability or provide interpretable, trustworthy scores.
AI.method: They fine-tune Qwen2-Audio-7B-Instruct with multi-rater human judgments using a rubric with three aspects—accuracy, fluency, and prosody—and model scores via Gaussian uncertainty-aware regression. They then apply conformal calibration to obtain well-calibrated, interpretable confidence intervals around predicted scores, comparing against standard regression and classification baselines.
AI.result: Their rubric-guided model with Gaussian uncertainty modeling plus conformal calibration achieves the best alignment with human ratings, especially for fluency and prosody, surpassing regression and classification baselines, while revealing that accuracy remains harder to assess reliably.
AI.conclusion: Encoding human rating rubrics into the model and explicitly modeling uncertainty yields more reliable, explainable SpeechLLM-based L2 speech assessment, showing a promising path to trustworthy automatic speaking evaluation that acknowledges natural rater variability.

title: LLM NL2SQL Robustness: Surface Noise vs. Linguistic Variation in Traditional and Agentic Settings
summary: Robustness evaluation for Natural Language to SQL (NL2SQL) systems is essential because real-world database environments are dynamic, noisy, and continuously evolving, whereas conventional benchmark evaluations typically assume static schemas and well-formed user inputs. In this work, we introduce a robustness evaluation benchmark containing approximately ten types of perturbations and conduct evaluations under both traditional and agentic settings. We assess multiple state-of-the-art large language models (LLMs), including Grok-4.1, Gemini-3-Pro, Claude-Opus-4.6, and GPT-5.2. Our results show that these models generally maintain strong performance under several perturbations; however, notable performance degradation is observed for surface-level noise (e.g., character-level corruption) and linguistic variation that preserves semantics while altering lexical or syntactic forms. Furthermore, we observe that surface-level noise causes larger performance drops in traditional pipelines, whereas linguistic variation presents greater challenges in agentic settings. These findings highlight the remaining challenges in achieving robust NL2SQL systems, particularly in handling linguistic variability.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a robustness benchmark for NL2SQL systems with about ten perturbation types, evaluating state-of-the-art LLMs in both traditional and agentic settings, and finds that while models are robust to some changes, they degrade notably under surface noise and linguistic variation.
AI.motivation: Conventional NL2SQL benchmarks assume clean inputs and fixed schemas, which is unrealistic for real-world databases that are noisy, dynamic, and evolving. There is a need to systematically understand how NL2SQL systems perform under various realistic perturbations to inputs and settings, especially as LLM-based systems become widely deployed.
AI.method: The authors design a robustness evaluation benchmark comprising roughly ten different perturbation types applied to NL2SQL tasks, spanning surface-level noise (e.g., character corruptions) and semantic-preserving linguistic variations (lexical/syntactic changes). They then evaluate several leading LLMs—Grok-4.1, Gemini-3-Pro, Claude-Opus-4.6, and GPT-5.2—under two evaluation regimes: a traditional single-shot pipeline and an agentic setting where models act as agents to accomplish the task.
AI.result: All evaluated LLMs maintain relatively strong performance under some perturbation types, but suffer marked accuracy drops when user queries contain character-level corruption or when they are rephrased in ways that change wording or syntax while preserving meaning. The type of perturbation interacts with the evaluation regime: surface-level noise harms traditional pipelines more, whereas semantic-preserving linguistic variations cause larger drops in agentic evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Current state-of-the-art LLM-based NL2SQL systems are not yet robust to realistic variation in user inputs, especially to linguistic variability and noisy text. Robustness issues manifest differently in traditional versus agentic setups, suggesting that both benchmark types are needed and that future work should prioritize methods for handling linguistic variation and noise to build resilient NL2SQL solutions.

title: Generative AI-assisted Participatory Modeling in Socio-Environmental Planning under Deep Uncertainty
summary: Socio-environmental planning under deep uncertainty requires researchers to identify and conceptualize problems before exploring policies and deploying plans. In practice and model-based planning approaches, this problem conceptualization process often relies on participatory modeling to translate stakeholders' natural-language descriptions into a quantitative model, making this process complex and time-consuming. To facilitate this process, we propose a templated workflow that uses large language models for an initial conceptualization process. During the workflow, researchers can use large language models to identify the essential model components from stakeholders' intuitive problem descriptions, explore their diverse perspectives approaching the problem, assemble these components into a unified model, and eventually implement the model in Python through iterative communication. These results will facilitate the subsequent socio-environmental planning under deep uncertainty steps. Using ChatGPT 5.2 Instant, we demonstrated this workflow on the lake problem and an electricity market problem, both of which demonstrate socio-environmental planning problems. In both cases, acceptable outputs were obtained after a few iterations with human verification and refinement. These experiments indicated that large language models can serve as an effective tool for facilitating participatory modeling in the problem conceptualization process in socio-environmental planning.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a templated workflow that uses large language models to speed up and structure the early, conceptual phase of participatory modeling in socio-environmental planning under deep uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Socio-environmental planning problems under deep uncertainty require careful, participatory problem conceptualization before any modeling or policy analysis. Existing practice translates stakeholders' natural-language descriptions into quantitative models manually, which is complex, iterative, and time-consuming. There is a need to make this early conceptualization more efficient and systematic without replacing human judgment.
AI.method: The authors design a structured workflow in which large language models (LLMs) are used as assistants during problem conceptualization. Given stakeholders' intuitive, natural-language descriptions of a planning problem, the LLM is prompted to: (1) extract essential model components; (2) articulate and compare diverse stakeholder perspectives; (3) assemble these components into a unified conceptual model; and (4) iteratively refine and implement this model as Python code through back-and-forth interaction with researchers. They test this workflow using ChatGPT 5.2 Instant on two benchmark socio-environmental planning problems: the lake problem and an electricity market problem.
AI.result: For both the lake problem and the electricity market problem, the workflow produced reasonable conceptual models and corresponding Python implementations after a few interactive refinement steps with human verification. The outputs were judged acceptable for subsequent use in socio-environmental planning under deep uncertainty, indicating that the templated workflow is practically usable.
AI.conclusion: Large language models, embedded in a structured, researcher-guided workflow, can substantially facilitate participatory modeling during the problem conceptualization phase of socio-environmental planning under deep uncertainty. While human oversight and refinement remain necessary, LLMs can effectively help identify key model components, integrate multiple perspectives, and translate natural-language problem descriptions into initial quantitative models, thereby reducing effort and time in early-stage modeling.

title: Evaluating Ill-Defined Tasks in Large Language Models
summary: Many evaluations of Large Language Models (LLMs) target tasks that are inherently ill-defined, with unclear input and output spaces and ambiguous success criteria. We analyze why existing evaluation benchmarks and metrics fail to provide reliable or diagnostic signals of model capability for such tasks. We examine two case studies: Complex Instruction Following (CIF), where we identify recurring issues including limited coverage of real-world instruction complexity, sensitivity to instruction phrasing, inconsistent and non-comparable metrics, and instability introduced by LLM-based judges; and Natural Language to Mermaid Sequence Diagrams (NL2Mermaid), where we show how multi-faceted evaluation criteria can yield actionable insights beyond aggregate scores. Together, these case studies show that current evaluations frequently conflate distinct failure modes, yielding scores that are unstable, non-diagnostic, and difficult to act upon. Our findings expose fundamental limitations in existing evaluation practices for ill-defined tasks and motivate more robust, interpretable evaluation designs.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques current ways of evaluating LLMs on ill-defined tasks, showing that popular benchmarks and metrics are unstable and non-diagnostic, and proposes more robust, interpretable evaluation designs using two case studies.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used for complex, real-world tasks, many evaluations rely on tasks with vague definitions, unclear success criteria, and noisy automatic or LLM-based judges. These evaluations often give misleading or unhelpful signals about what models can actually do or how to improve them. The authors want to systematically understand why current evaluations fail on such ill-defined tasks and how to design better, more informative evaluation protocols.
AI.method: The authors conduct a critical analysis via two in-depth case studies. For Complex Instruction Following (CIF), they review and dissect existing benchmarks and metrics, identifying issues like limited coverage of realistic instruction complexity, high sensitivity to prompt wording, lack of standardized and comparable metrics, and instability introduced by using LLMs as automatic judges. For the Natural Language to Mermaid Sequence Diagrams (NL2Mermaid) task, they design or examine an evaluation with multiple distinct criteria (e.g., structural correctness, faithfulness to the description, completeness) to show how multi-dimensional metrics can provide more actionable diagnostic information than a single aggregate score. They qualitatively and quantitatively compare these setups to illustrate failure modes and improvements.
AI.result: For CIF, they find that current benchmarks and metrics are brittle: scores vary significantly with small changes in instruction phrasing, benchmarks underrepresent the true variety and complexity of real-world instructions, metrics are inconsistent across works so results are not comparable, and LLM-based judges introduce additional variance and bias. For NL2Mermaid, they demonstrate that decomposing evaluation into several targeted criteria reveals different types of model errors (e.g., structural vs. semantic vs. formatting) that would be obscured by a single overall score. This multi-faceted evaluation yields more stable, interpretable signals of model performance and clearer guidance for model development.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that many current LLM evaluation practices for ill-defined tasks conflate different failure modes into unstable, non-diagnostic scores. This makes it hard to trust reported performance or to understand how to improve models. The authors argue that evaluations must be redesigned to be more robust and interpretable: covering realistic task complexity, reducing sensitivity to superficial variations, standardizing metrics, and using multi-dimensional, task-specific criteria rather than single aggregate scores. Their case studies illustrate both the pitfalls of existing CIF evaluations and the benefits of more structured, decomposed evaluation frameworks like the one used for NL2Mermaid.

title: Transformers are Bayesian Networks
summary:
Transformers are the dominant architecture in AI, yet why they work remains poorly understood. This paper offers a precise answer: a transformer is a Bayesian network. We establish this in five ways.
  First, we prove that every sigmoid transformer with any weights implements weighted loopy belief propagation on its implicit factor graph. One layer is one round of BP. This holds for any weights -- trained, random, or constructed. Formally verified against standard mathematical axioms.
  Second, we give a constructive proof that a transformer can implement exact belief propagation on any declared knowledge base. On knowledge bases without circular dependencies this yields provably correct probability estimates at every node. Formally verified against standard mathematical axioms.
  Third, we prove uniqueness: a sigmoid transformer that produces exact posteriors necessarily has BP weights. There is no other path through the sigmoid architecture to exact posteriors. Formally verified against standard mathematical axioms.
  Fourth, we delineate the AND/OR boolean structure of the transformer layer: attention is AND, the FFN is OR, and their strict alternation is Pearl's gather/update algorithm exactly.
  Fifth, we confirm all formal results experimentally, corroborating the Bayesian network characterization in practice. We also establish the practical viability of loopy belief propagation despite the current lack of a theoretical convergence guarantee.
  We further prove that verifiable inference requires a finite concept space. Any finite verification procedure can distinguish at most finitely many concepts. Without grounding, correctness is not defined. Hallucination is not a bug that scaling can fix. It is the structural consequence of operating without concepts. Formally verified against standard mathematical axioms.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that transformers are best understood as Bayesian networks performing belief propagation, and shows this theoretically and experimentally, then connects this to limits of verifiable inference and hallucinations.
AI.motivation: Despite transformers’ empirical success, we lack a precise, mechanistic theoretical understanding of why they work. The authors aim to give a rigorous, axiomatized explanation of transformers as probabilistic inference machines, and to clarify fundamental limits of correctness and hallucination in large models.
AI.method: They formally prove equivalences between sigmoid transformers and (loopy) belief propagation on implicit factor graphs: (1) a general proof that any sigmoid transformer layer implements one iteration of weighted loopy BP; (2) a constructive proof that transformers can implement exact BP on arbitrary knowledge bases; (3) a uniqueness proof that exact posterior computation forces BP-like weights; (4) a structural analysis mapping attention to AND, FFN to OR, and their alternation to Pearl’s gather/update algorithm; (5) experimental validations of these formal claims and an analysis of loopy BP’s practical viability. They also provide formal arguments about finite concept spaces and verification limits.
AI.result: They show that sigmoid transformers are mathematically equivalent to Bayesian networks performing belief propagation, both in approximate (loopy) and exact forms, and that exact posterior computation in this architecture uniquely corresponds to BP-style weights. They demonstrate the AND/OR semantics of attention and FFNs and experimentally verify that transformers behave as predicted by the Bayesian network interpretation, including practical effectiveness of loopy BP. They further prove that any finite verification procedure can only distinguish finitely many concepts, implying that correctness of inferences requires a grounded, finite concept space.
AI.conclusion: Transformers can be precisely characterized as Bayesian networks executing (loopy) belief propagation, with each layer corresponding to a BP iteration and attention/FFN implementing Pearl’s gather/update process. Exact probabilistic inference within this architecture uniquely corresponds to BP-structured weights. Moreover, verifiable, non-hallucinatory inference requires grounded, finite concept spaces; without such grounding, hallucination is a structural property rather than a fixable artifact of scale.

title: Large Reasoning Models Struggle to Transfer Parametric Knowledge Across Scripts
summary: In this work, we analyze shortcomings in cross-lingual knowledge transfer in large, modern reasoning LLMs. We demonstrate that the perceived gap in knowledge transfer is primarily a script barrier. First, we conduct an observational data analysis on the performance of thinking models on two datasets with local knowledge from around the world, ECLeKTic and MultiLoKo. Our regression analysis shows that script match - not language or family - is the primary predictor of knowledge transfer failure once model capability and question difficulty are accounted for. We further this finding by providing the LLMs with the key entities of the questions in their source language and find that this disproportionately improves cross-script questions. We then posit that these LLMs could be reasoning better at test-time. To evaluate this, we develop a synthetic generation pipeline to design SFT samples to encourage the model to better reason about transliteration ambiguities when trying to fetch parametric knowledge at inference-time. We show that teaching two models to reason better reduces the cross-script transfer gap. As a result, we conclude that there is potential to improve cross-lingual parametric knowledge transfer during post-training.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why large reasoning LLMs struggle to transfer knowledge across languages and finds that script differences, rather than language itself, are the main barrier, and shows that targeted training can reduce this gap.
AI.motivation: Large reasoning LLMs are expected to transfer factual and parametric knowledge across languages, but in practice their performance drops, especially when question and training languages differ. Existing explanations often focus on language or linguistic distance, but the authors suspect that the writing system (script) itself is a major bottleneck. Understanding this failure mode is important to build truly multilingual models and to design interventions that improve cross-lingual knowledge access without full retraining.
AI.method:
1) Perform observational data analysis on two multilingual local-knowledge benchmarks (ECLeKTic and MultiLoKo) using modern "thinking" LLMs.
2) Run regression analyses controlling for model capability and question difficulty to identify what factors predict failures in knowledge transfer, with particular attention to script match vs. language family.
3) Augment test-time prompts by providing key entities of each question in their source language and measure the performance gains, especially for cross-script vs same-script questions.
4) Propose that the models could reason more effectively at test time about transliteration and entity matching, and design a synthetic data generation pipeline to create SFT (supervised fine-tuning) samples that explicitly train models to handle transliteration ambiguities when retrieving parametric knowledge.
5) Fine-tune two models with this synthetic data and evaluate how this affects cross-script knowledge transfer performance and the cross-script performance gap.
AI.result: The regression analysis reveals that script mismatch is the dominant predictor of cross-lingual knowledge transfer failures, once model ability and question difficulty are taken into account; language or language family play a much smaller role. Supplying key entities in the original source language significantly improves accuracy, with disproportionately larger gains for questions where the source and target use different scripts. After SFT with the proposed synthetic reasoning-oriented data, the two tested models show reduced performance gaps between same-script and cross-script settings, indicating better handling of transliteration and cross-script entity resolution.
AI.conclusion: The main barrier to cross-lingual parametric knowledge transfer in large reasoning LLMs is not language per se but script mismatch, which interferes with recognizing and retrieving the right knowledge. However, this weakness is at least partially addressable during post-training: by explicitly teaching LLMs to reason about transliteration and cross-script entity representations via synthetic SFT data, it is possible to narrow the cross-script transfer gap. This suggests that improved post-training strategies can substantially enhance multilingual knowledge access without altering pretraining data or architecture.

title: Cascade-Aware Multi-Agent Routing: Spatio-Temporal Sidecars and Geometry-Switching
summary:
A common architectural pattern in advanced AI reasoning systems is the symbolic graph network: specialized agents or modules connected by delegation edges, routing tasks through a dynamic execution graph. Current schedulers optimize load and fitness but are geometry-blind: they do not model how failures propagate differently in tree-like versus cyclic regimes. In tree-like delegation, a single failure can cascade exponentially; in dense cyclic graphs, failures tend to self-limit. We identify this observability gap, quantify its system-level cost, and propose a lightweight mitigation.
  We formulate online geometry control for route-risk estimation on time-indexed execution graphs with route-local failure history. Our approach combines (i) a Euclidean spatio-temporal propagation baseline, (ii) a hyperbolic route-risk model with temporal decay (and optional burst excitation), and (iii) a learned geometry selector over structural features. The selector is a compact MLP (9->12->1) using six topology statistics plus three geometry-aware signals: BFS shell-growth slope, cycle-rank norm, and fitted Poincare curvature. On the Genesis 3 benchmark distribution, adaptive switching improves win rate in the hardest non_tree regime from 64-72% (fixed hyperbolic variants) to 92%, and achieves 87.2% overall win rate.
  To measure total system value, we compare against Genesis 3 routing without any spatio-temporal sidecar, using only native bandit/LinUCB signals (team fitness and mean node load). This baseline achieves 50.4% win rate overall and 20% in tree-like regimes; the full sidecar recovers 87.2% overall (+36.8 pp), with +48 to +68 pp gains in tree-like settings, consistent with a cascade-sensitivity analysis. Overall, a 133-parameter sidecar substantially mitigates geometry-blind failure propagation in one high-capability execution-graph system.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a small "geometry-aware" scheduling sidecar for symbolic graph networks that models how failures propagate differently in tree-like vs cyclic execution graphs, dramatically improving success rates over geometry-blind schedulers.
AI.motivation: Existing schedulers for advanced AI reasoning systems treat the execution graph as geometry-agnostic, optimizing only load and local fitness. They ignore that the same local failure can have very different global impact depending on whether the delegation graph is tree-like or densely cyclic. This geometry-blindness causes unobserved and costly cascades of failures, especially in tree-like regimes. The authors aim to make routing decisions that are aware of the graph’s structural failure dynamics while remaining lightweight enough to be deployed as a sidecar module.
AI.method: The authors formalize an online geometry control problem for estimating route risk on time-indexed execution graphs, conditioned on local failure histories. They introduce a spatio-temporal sidecar composed of three key elements: (i) a Euclidean baseline model for failure propagation over space and time; (ii) a hyperbolic route-risk model with temporal decay and optional burst excitation to better capture cascade-like behaviors; and (iii) a learned geometry-selector network that chooses which geometry to trust for a given situation. The selector is a compact MLP (input 9, hidden 12, output 1) that takes six topological statistics plus three geometry-aware signals—BFS shell-growth slope, cycle-rank norm, and a fitted Poincaré curvature estimate—to adaptively switch between Euclidean and hyperbolic failure models online.
AI.result: On the Genesis 3 benchmark distribution, the adaptive geometry selector yields large gains in routing outcomes. In the hardest non-tree regime, adaptive switching raises win rate from 64–72% (for the best fixed hyperbolic variants) to 92%. Overall win rate reaches 87.2%. When compared to a baseline Genesis 3 routing setup that relies only on standard bandit/LinUCB indicators (team fitness and mean node load) and uses no spatio-temporal sidecar, performance improves from 50.4% to 87.2% overall, with particularly strong gains of +48 to +68 percentage points in tree-like regimes where cascades are most damaging.
AI.conclusion: A small, 133-parameter geometry-aware sidecar that models failure propagation in different graph geometries can substantially reduce the cost of geometry-blind scheduling in symbolic graph networks. By dynamically selecting between Euclidean and hyperbolic failure models based on structural and curvature-related features of the execution graph, the system mitigates cascading failures—especially in tree-like delegation structures—and yields large win-rate improvements on a strong execution-graph benchmark. The results suggest that incorporating explicit graph-geometry awareness into schedulers is a high-leverage, low-overhead way to improve robustness in complex AI reasoning systems.

title: Ensemble Self-Training for Unsupervised Machine Translation
summary: We present an ensemble-driven self-training framework for unsupervised neural machine translation (UNMT). Starting from a primary language pair, we train multiple UNMT models that share the same translation task but differ in an auxiliary language, inducing structured diversity across models. We then generate pseudo-translations for the primary pair using token-level ensemble decoding, averaging model predictions in both directions. These ensemble outputs are used as synthetic parallel data to further train each model, allowing the models to improve via shared supervision. At deployment time, we select a single model by validation performance, preserving single-model inference cost. Experiments show statistically significant improvements over single-model UNMT baselines, with mean gains of 1.7 chrF when translating from English and 0.67 chrF when translating into English.

AI.tldr: They propose an ensemble-based self-training framework to improve unsupervised neural machine translation (UNMT) by using multiple auxiliary-language models and ensemble decoding to create better synthetic parallel data.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised NMT suffers from weaker performance compared to supervised systems, partly due to low-quality synthetic parallel data and under-utilization of diverse model predictions. The authors aim to improve UNMT quality while keeping deployment costs low, without requiring human-labeled parallel corpora.
AI.method: They start with a primary language pair (e.g., English–X) and train several UNMT models that all translate this pair but each is coupled with a different auxiliary language, encouraging diverse internal representations. At training time, they perform token-level ensemble decoding over these models in both translation directions to generate pseudo-translations, averaging the models’ token probability distributions. The resulting high-quality ensemble outputs serve as synthetic parallel data to further train each individual model in a self-training loop. At inference, they pick the best single model based on validation performance so runtime cost remains that of a single UNMT model.
AI.result: Across experiments, the proposed framework yields statistically significant performance gains compared to standard single-model UNMT baselines. On average, they report improvements of about 1.7 chrF points for translations from English into other languages and about 0.67 chrF points for translations into English from other languages.
AI.conclusion: Using structured model diversity via auxiliary languages combined with ensemble-based pseudo-label generation and self-training can meaningfully improve unsupervised NMT, while still allowing single-model inference at deployment. This suggests that ensemble-driven self-training is an effective way to enhance UNMT without increasing test-time computational cost.

title: How Clued up are LLMs? Evaluating Multi-Step Deductive Reasoning in a Text-Based Game Environment
summary: Deducing whodunit proves challenging for LLM agents. In this paper, we implement a text-based multi-agent version of the classic board game Clue as a rule-based testbed for evaluating multi-step deductive reasoning, with six agents drawn from GPT-4o-mini and Gemini-2.5-Flash. We further investigate whether fine-tuning on structured logic puzzles transfers to improved in-game reasoning and gameplay. Across 18 simulated games, agents achieve only four correct wins, indicating difficulty in maintaining consistent deductive reasoning over the course of a full game. Additionally, we find that fine-tuning does not reliably improve performance and, in some cases, appears to increase reasoning volume without improving reasoning precision.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well large language model (LLM) agents perform multi-step deductive reasoning in a text-based, multi-agent implementation of the Clue board game, and finds they struggle to maintain consistent logic, with fine-tuning on logic puzzles offering little reliable benefit.
AI.motivation: LLMs often appear strong at short, local reasoning tasks but their ability to sustain correct, multi-step deductive reasoning over longer interactive scenarios is unclear. A structured, rule-driven environment like the game Clue provides a natural testbed for studying how well LLM agents can track information, update beliefs, and make deductions in a multi-agent, multi-step setting. The authors want to stress-test reasoning consistency and examine whether targeted fine-tuning on structured logic puzzles can meaningfully improve such in-game reasoning.
AI.method: The authors implement a text-based, rule-based multi-agent version of the board game Clue, designed specifically as a deductive reasoning benchmark. They populate the game with six LLM agents instantiated from GPT-4o-mini and Gemini-2.5-Flash. The agents play 18 simulated games under a fixed protocol. In addition, some agents are fine-tuned on structured logic puzzle datasets to test whether this training transfers to better gameplay reasoning. They analyze win rates, correctness of deductions, and qualitative properties such as reasoning volume versus reasoning precision.
AI.result: Across 18 simulated games, the LLM agents collectively achieve only four correct wins, indicating generally poor performance at maintaining coherent deductive chains and making correct final accusations over an extended game. Fine-tuned agents do not show consistent performance gains; in some cases, fine-tuning leads to more verbose reasoning traces without corresponding improvements in actual deduction accuracy, suggesting a mismatch between surface-level reasoning behavior and underlying logical correctness.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that current LLM agents, even when using strong modern models and multi-agent setups, struggle with sustained, multi-step deductive reasoning in a realistic, rule-based setting like Clue. Fine-tuning on structured logic puzzles does not reliably translate into better in-game reasoning, and may instead encourage more verbose but not more accurate explanations. These findings highlight a gap between apparent reasoning fluency and genuine deductive competence, and underscore the need for new methods or architectures to improve long-horizon, structured reasoning in LLM agents.

title: Evaluating LLM-Simulated Conversations in Modeling Inconsistent and Uncollaborative Behaviors in Human Social Interaction
summary: Simulating human conversations using large language models (LLMs) has emerged as a scalable methodology for modeling human social interaction. However, simulating human conversations is challenging because they inherently involve inconsistent and uncollaborative behaviors, such as misunderstandings and interruptions. Analysis comparing inconsistent and uncollaborative behaviors in human- and LLM-generated conversations remains limited, although reproducing these behaviors is integral to simulating human-like and complex social interaction. In this work, we introduce CoCoEval, an evaluation framework that analyzes LLM-simulated conversations by detecting 10 types of inconsistent and uncollaborative behaviors at the turn level using an LLM-as-a-Judge. Using CoCoEval, we evaluate GPT-4.1, GPT-5.1, and Claude Opus 4 by comparing the frequencies of detected behaviors in conversations simulated by each model and in human conversations across academic, business, and governmental meetings, as well as debates. Our analysis shows that (1) under vanilla prompting, LLM-simulated conversations exhibit far fewer inconsistent and uncollaborative behaviors than human conversations; (2) prompt engineering does not provide reliable control over these behaviors, as our results show that different prompts lead to their under- or overproduction; and (3) supervised fine-tuning on human conversations can lead LLMs to overproduce a narrow set of behaviors, such as repetition. Our findings highlight the difficulty of simulating human conversations, raising concerns about the use of LLMs as a proxy for human social interaction.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CoCoEval, a framework to automatically evaluate how well LLM-simulated conversations reproduce human-like inconsistent and uncollaborative behaviors, and shows that current LLMs exhibit such behaviors far less (or in skewed ways) than human conversations, challenging their use as proxies for human social interaction.
AI.motivation: Existing work uses LLM-generated conversations to model human social interaction, but real human conversations contain misunderstandings, interruptions, contradictions, and other inconsistent or uncollaborative behaviors. These aspects are essential to realistic social dynamics, yet prior analyses comparing such behaviors in human vs LLM conversations are limited. There is a need for a systematic way to detect and quantify these behaviors to understand how human-like LLM conversations really are and how controllable these behaviors are via prompting or fine-tuning.
AI.method: The authors introduce CoCoEval, an evaluation framework that uses an LLM-as-a-Judge to detect 10 predefined categories of inconsistent and uncollaborative behaviors at the turn level in multi-party conversations. They collect or use corpora of human conversations (academic, business, governmental meetings, and debates) and generate parallel LLM-simulated conversations with multiple frontier models (GPT-4.1, GPT-5.1, Claude Opus 4) under various prompting strategies and with/without supervised fine-tuning on human data. They then apply CoCoEval to compare the frequency and distribution of these behaviors across human and LLM conversations and across conditions.
AI.result: The evaluation shows: (1) With standard (vanilla) prompting, LLM-simulated conversations contain substantially fewer inconsistent and uncollaborative behaviors than human conversations. (2) Prompt engineering fails to provide stable, fine-grained control over these behaviors; depending on the specific prompt, LLMs either underproduce or overproduce certain behavior types relative to humans. (3) Supervised fine-tuning on human conversations can cause LLMs to overproduce a narrow subset of behaviors, such as repetitive turns, without achieving a balanced, human-like spectrum of behaviors. Overall, even advanced LLMs do not match the complexity and messiness of genuine human conversational dynamics.
AI.conclusion: CoCoEval reveals that current LLMs struggle to faithfully reproduce the full range and realistic distribution of inconsistent and uncollaborative behaviors seen in human conversation. Simple prompting or straightforward supervised fine-tuning are insufficient to close this gap and can introduce new distortions. These findings cast doubt on the reliability of using LLM-simulated conversations as stand-ins for human social interaction in research or downstream applications and point to a need for more nuanced modeling and control of conversational behavior in LLMs.

title: AI Scientist via Synthetic Task Scaling
summary: With the advent of AI agents, automatic scientific discovery has become a tenable goal. Many recent works scaffold agentic systems that can perform machine learning research, but don't offer a principled way to train such agents -- and current LLMs often generate plausible-looking but ineffective ideas. To make progress on training agents that can learn from doing, we provide a novel synthetic environment generation pipeline targeting machine learning agents. Our pipeline automatically synthesizes machine learning challenges compatible with the SWE-agent framework, covering topic sampling, dataset proposal, and code generation. The resulting synthetic tasks are 1) grounded in real machine learning datasets, because the proposed datasets are verified against the Huggingface API and are 2) verified for higher quality with a self-debugging loop. To validate the effectiveness of our synthetic tasks, we tackle MLGym, a benchmark for machine learning tasks. From the synthetic tasks, we sample trajectories from a teacher model (GPT-5), then use the trajectories to train a student model (Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B). The student models trained with our synthetic tasks achieve improved performance on MLGym, raising the AUP metric by 9% for Qwen3-4B and 12% for Qwen3-8B.

AI.tldr: They build a pipeline that automatically generates realistic machine-learning research tasks for AI agents and show that training on these synthetic tasks improves small models’ performance on a machine-learning benchmark.
AI.motivation: Existing AI research agents can propose ML ideas but often produce plausible-sounding yet low-quality research; moreover, there is no principled, scalable way to train such agents on diverse, realistic ML tasks. The authors aim to create an automatic environment where agents can repeatedly practice and learn from actually doing machine-learning experimentation, in a way that is grounded in real datasets and systematically verifiable.
AI.method: They design a synthetic environment generation pipeline compatible with the SWE-agent framework. The pipeline: (1) samples ML topics; (2) proposes datasets and verifies them via the Hugging Face API to ensure they are real and accessible; and (3) generates the accompanying code and task setup. A self-debugging loop then checks and improves the generated tasks for correctness and quality. Using this pipeline, they construct synthetic machine-learning tasks and collect teacher trajectories from a strong model (GPT-5) solving these tasks. These trajectories are used to train smaller student models (Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B).
AI.result: The pipeline produces synthetic ML tasks that are both grounded in real datasets and quality-checked by a self-debugging procedure. When student models (Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B) are trained on trajectories collected from GPT-5 solving these tasks, their performance on the MLGym benchmark improves, specifically achieving 9% and 12% gains in the AUP metric, respectively.
AI.conclusion: Automatically generated, dataset-grounded ML tasks can serve as an effective training curriculum for research agents. The proposed synthetic environment pipeline not only creates higher-quality tasks via verification and self-debugging but also yields measurable performance improvements on an external benchmark, suggesting that such task generation is a promising strategy for training machine-learning research agents that learn by doing.

title: Knowledge Localization in Mixture-of-Experts LLMs Using Cross-Lingual Inconsistency
summary: Modern LLMs continue to exhibit significant variance in behavior across languages, such as being able to recall factual information in some languages but not others. While typically studied as a problem to be mitigated, in this work, we propose leveraging this cross-lingual inconsistency as a tool for interpretability in mixture-of-experts (MoE) LLMs. Our knowledge localization framework contrasts routing for sets of languages where the model correctly recalls information from languages where it fails. This allows us to isolate model components that play a functional role in answering about a piece of knowledge. Our method proceeds in two stages: (1) querying the model with difficult factual questions across a diverse set of languages to generate "success" and "failure" activation buckets and then (2) applying a statistical contrastive analysis to the MoE router logits to identify experts important for knowledge. To validate the necessity of this small number of experts for answering a knowledge question, we deactivate them and re-ask the question. We find that despite only deactivating about 20 out of 6000 experts, the model no longer answers correctly in over 40% of cases. Generally, this method provides a realistic and scalable knowledge localization approach to address increasingly complex LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method to localize where factual knowledge is stored in mixture-of-experts LLMs by exploiting cross-lingual inconsistencies in question answering, identifying a small subset of experts that are functionally necessary for specific facts.
AI.motivation: Modern large language models behave inconsistently across languages, especially in factual recall. Instead of treating this only as a weakness, the authors see it as a natural experimental perturbation that can reveal internal mechanisms. As MoE models become larger and more complex, there is a growing need for scalable interpretability methods that can pinpoint which model components are responsible for specific knowledge, beyond crude layer- or neuron-level analyses.
AI.method: The method has two main stages. First, the model is asked difficult factual questions in many languages, and for each question, languages where the model answers correctly form a "success" bucket while languages where it fails form a "failure" bucket. Second, the authors perform a statistical contrastive analysis on the MoE router logits between these success and failure buckets to find experts whose routing patterns differ systematically and are thus implicated in recalling that knowledge. To test causality, they then selectively deactivate (turn off) the identified experts and re-ask the original question.
AI.result: The analysis identifies a small subset of experts—about 20 out of a total of 6000—that are strongly associated with correctly answering specific factual questions. When these experts are deactivated, the model’s accuracy on those questions drops sharply: in more than 40% of tested cases, the model no longer produces the correct answer, despite the vast majority of experts remaining active.
AI.conclusion: Cross-lingual inconsistency can be productively used as a natural lever for interpretability in MoE LLMs. By contrasting routing patterns between languages that succeed and fail on the same factual queries, the authors provide a scalable method to localize knowledge to a small number of experts and show these experts are functionally necessary for many questions. This offers a more realistic way to study knowledge storage and utilization in increasingly large and complex LLM architectures.

title: Draft-and-Prune: Improving the Reliability of Auto-formalization for Logical Reasoning
summary: Auto-formalization (AF) translates natural-language reasoning problems into solver-executable programs, enabling symbolic solvers to perform sound logical deduction. In practice, however, AF pipelines are currently brittle: programs may fail to execute, or execute but encode incorrect semantics. While prior work largely mitigates syntactic failures via repairs based on solver feedback, reducing semantics failures remains a major bottleneck. We propose Draft-and-Prune (D&P), an inference-time framework that improves AF-based logical reasoning via diversity and verification. D&P first drafts multiple natural-language plans and conditions program generation on them. It further prunes executable but contradictory or ambiguous formalizations, and aggregates predictions from surviving paths via majority voting. Across four representative benchmarks (AR-LSAT, ProofWriter, PrOntoQA, LogicalDeduction), D&P substantially strengthens AF-based reasoning without extra supervision. On AR-LSAT, in the AF-only setting, D&P achieves 78.43% accuracy with GPT-4 and 78.00% accuracy with GPT-4o, significantly outperforming the strongest AF baselines MAD-LOGIC and CLOVER. D&P then attains near-ceiling performance on the other benchmarks, including 100% on PrOntoQA and LogicalDeduction.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Draft-and-Prune (D&P), an inference-time framework that improves auto-formalization-based logical reasoning by generating diverse plans, pruning incorrect formal programs, and aggregating the remaining solutions, achieving state-of-the-art results on several reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Auto-formalization can, in principle, let symbolic solvers perform sound logical deduction from natural language, but current pipelines are brittle: many generated programs either fail to execute or encode the wrong semantics. Existing work mainly handles syntactic errors using solver feedback, leaving semantic errors as a major bottleneck. The authors are motivated to reduce these semantic failures and improve the reliability and accuracy of AF-based logical reasoning without requiring extra supervision or retraining.
AI.method: The authors propose Draft-and-Prune (D&P), an inference-time framework built around diversity and verification. First, the system drafts multiple natural-language reasoning plans for a given problem. Program generation is then conditioned on each plan, yielding multiple formalized candidate programs. Next, it prunes candidates that are executable but produce contradictory or ambiguous results, effectively filtering out many semantic errors. Finally, D&P aggregates predictions from the remaining consistent programs using majority voting to obtain the final answer. This framework wraps around existing AF pipelines and uses only inference-time techniques rather than additional training data.
AI.result: On four representative logical reasoning benchmarks—AR-LSAT, ProofWriter, PrOntoQA, and LogicalDeduction—D&P significantly improves performance over existing auto-formalization baselines. In an AF-only setting on AR-LSAT, D&P achieves 78.43% accuracy with GPT-4 and 78.00% with GPT-4o, surpassing strong baselines such as MAD-LOGIC and CLOVER. On the other three benchmarks, D&P reaches near-ceiling performance, including 100% accuracy on PrOntoQA and LogicalDeduction. These results demonstrate that D&P substantially strengthens AF-based reasoning without any extra supervision.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that Draft-and-Prune is an effective, supervision-free, inference-time framework for improving the robustness and accuracy of auto-formalization-based logical reasoning. By leveraging diverse planning, semantic pruning of formal programs, and majority-vote aggregation, D&P mitigates both syntactic and semantic failures in AF pipelines. Its strong empirical performance across multiple benchmarks suggests that structured diversity plus verification is a powerful pattern for enhancing AF systems and may generalize to other reasoning tasks and model architectures.

title: Exploiting the English Grammar Profile for L2 grammatical analysis with LLMs
summary: Evaluating the grammatical competence of second language (L2) learners is essential both for providing targeted feedback and for assessing proficiency. To achieve this, we propose a novel framework leveraging the English Grammar Profile (EGP), a taxonomy of grammatical constructs mapped to the proficiency levels of the Common European Framework of Reference (CEFR), to detect learners' attempts at grammatical constructs and classify them as successful or unsuccessful. This detection can then be used to provide fine-grained feedback. Moreover, the grammatical constructs are used as predictors of proficiency assessment by using automatically detected attempts as predictors of holistic CEFR proficiency. For the selection of grammatical constructs derived from the EGP, rule-based and LLM-based classifiers are compared. We show that LLMs outperform rule-based methods on semantically and pragmatically nuanced constructs, while rule-based approaches remain competitive for constructs that rely purely on morphological or syntactic features and do not require semantic interpretation. For proficiency assessment, we evaluate both rule-based and hybrid pipelines and show that a hybrid approach combining a rule-based pre-filter with an LLM consistently yields the strongest performance. Since our framework operates on pairs of original learner sentences and their corrected counterparts, we also evaluate a fully automated pipeline using automatic grammatical error correction. This pipeline closely approaches the performance of semi-automated systems based on manual corrections, particularly for the detection of successful attempts at grammatical constructs. Overall, our framework emphasises learners' successful attempts in addition to unsuccessful ones, enabling positive, formative feedback and providing actionable insights into grammatical development.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a framework that uses the English Grammar Profile and both rule-based and LLM-based classifiers to detect and evaluate L2 learners’ grammatical construct attempts, and then uses these construct-level features to predict CEFR proficiency, with a hybrid rule-based+LLM pipeline performing best and supporting fine-grained, positive feedback.
AI.motivation: Evaluating second-language learners’ grammatical competence typically focuses on errors or holistic scores, offering limited insight for fine-grained feedback and detailed proficiency assessment. There is a need for a systematic way to (1) identify which specific grammar structures learners are attempting, (2) determine whether those attempts are successful or not, and (3) link these observations to standardized proficiency scales such as CEFR. Existing rule-based systems may struggle with semantically nuanced constructs, while purely LLM-based approaches may lack structure or interpretability. The paper aims to bridge this gap with a structured, CEFR-aligned, and practically useful framework.
AI.method: The authors build a framework grounded in the English Grammar Profile (EGP), which maps detailed grammatical constructs to CEFR levels. For each learner sentence and its corrected counterpart, they detect attempts at specific grammatical constructs and classify them as successful or unsuccessful. They implement and compare (a) rule-based classifiers and (b) LLM-based classifiers for detecting and labeling these constructs. They then aggregate the automatically detected construct attempts as features for predicting holistic CEFR proficiency, comparing fully rule-based pipelines with hybrid pipelines that combine a rule-based pre-filter and LLM-based classification. Finally, they test a fully automated setting where the corrected sentences are generated by an automatic grammatical error correction system instead of human annotators, and evaluate performance on construct detection and proficiency prediction.
AI.result: LLM-based classifiers outperform rule-based methods for constructs that require semantic or pragmatic interpretation, such as those where context or meaning must be understood beyond surface form. Rule-based approaches remain competitive for constructs that can be reliably identified from morphological or syntactic patterns alone, without deep semantic analysis. For CEFR proficiency prediction, hybrid pipelines that apply a rule-based pre-filter before using an LLM consistently achieve the strongest performance, surpassing purely rule-based pipelines. The fully automatic pipeline using automatic grammatical error correction produces results close to semi-automatic pipelines using manual corrections, especially for identifying successful attempts at grammatical constructs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed EGP-based framework effectively detects both successful and unsuccessful attempts at specific grammatical constructs in L2 learner writing and uses these as informative predictors of CEFR proficiency. Combining rule-based and LLM-based components yields the best trade-off between precision on structurally clear constructs and flexibility on semantically nuanced ones. The near-parity of automatic error-correction-based pipelines with those relying on manual corrections suggests the approach is scalable and practical. By explicitly modeling successful grammatical use, the framework supports more positive, formative feedback and offers detailed insights into learners’ grammatical development rather than focusing solely on errors.

title: Graph-Native Cognitive Memory for AI Agents: Formal Belief Revision Semantics for Versioned Memory Architectures
summary: While individual components for AI agent memory exist in prior systems, their architectural synthesis and formal grounding remain underexplored. We present Kumiho, a graph-native cognitive memory architecture grounded in formal belief revision semantics. The structural primitives required for cognitive memory -- immutable revisions, mutable tag pointers, typed dependency edges, URI-based addressing -- are identical to those required for managing agent-produced work as versionable assets, enabling a unified graph-native architecture that serves both purposes. The central formal contribution is a correspondence between the AGM belief revision framework and the operational semantics of a property graph memory system, proving satisfaction of the basic AGM postulates (K*2--K*6) and Hansson's belief base postulates (Relevance, Core-Retainment). The architecture implements a dual-store model (Redis working memory, Neo4j long-term graph) with hybrid fulltext and vector retrieval. On LoCoMo (token-level F1), Kumiho achieves 0.565 overall F1 (n=1,986) including 97.5% adversarial refusal accuracy. On LoCoMo-Plus, a Level-2 cognitive memory benchmark testing implicit constraint recall, Kumiho achieves 93.3% judge accuracy (n=401); independent reproduction by the benchmark authors yielded results in the mid-80% range, still substantially outperforming all published baselines (best: Gemini 2.5 Pro, 45.7%). Three architectural innovations drive the results: prospective indexing (LLM-generated future-scenario implications indexed at write time), event extraction (structured causal events preserved in summaries), and client-side LLM reranking. The architecture is model-decoupled: switching the answer model from GPT-4o-mini (~88%) to GPT-4o (93.3%) improves end-to-end accuracy without pipeline changes, at a total evaluation cost of ~$14 for 401 entries.

AI.tldr: Kumiho is a graph-native cognitive memory architecture for AI agents, formally grounded in belief revision theory, that unifies agent memory and artifact versioning and achieves state-of-the-art results on cognitive memory benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing AI agent systems have partial memory components but lack a unified, formally grounded architecture that handles both cognitive memory (beliefs, constraints, context) and management of agent-produced artifacts as versionable assets. There is also a gap between theoretical belief revision frameworks (like AGM) and concrete, operational memory systems for LLM-based agents. The authors aim to bridge this gap by designing a memory architecture that is both practically effective and backed by formal semantics, and that can outperform current baselines on challenging cognitive memory benchmarks, including adversarial and implicit-constraint tasks.
AI.method: The authors design Kumiho, a graph-native memory architecture based on property graphs with specific structural primitives: immutable revisions, mutable tag pointers, typed dependency edges, and URI-based addressing. They establish a formal correspondence between the AGM belief revision framework and the operational semantics of this graph memory, demonstrating that it satisfies core AGM postulates (K*2–K*6) and Hansson’s belief base postulates (Relevance, Core-Retainment). Implementation-wise, Kumiho uses a dual-store setup with Redis for working memory and Neo4j for long-term memory, combining full-text and vector retrieval. Three main techniques support performance: (1) prospective indexing, where the LLM generates possible future implications of information at write time and these are indexed; (2) event extraction, where structured causal events are extracted and preserved in summaries; and (3) client-side LLM-based reranking of retrieved items. The system is model-decoupled, allowing different LLMs to be swapped as answer models without changing the pipeline.
AI.result: On the LoCoMo benchmark using token-level F1, Kumiho obtains an overall F1 of 0.565 on 1,986 examples and shows strong robustness with 97.5% accuracy on adversarial refusal cases. On LoCoMo-Plus, a level-2 cognitive memory benchmark focused on implicit constraint recall, Kumiho reaches 93.3% judge accuracy on 401 examples using GPT-4o as the answer model. Independent reproduction by the benchmark authors achieves mid-80% accuracy, still far above all prior published baselines, where the previous best system (Gemini 2.5 Pro) achieved 45.7%. Swapping the answer model from GPT-4o-mini to GPT-4o improves overall accuracy from roughly 88% to 93.3% without architectural changes, with a total evaluation cost of about $14 for 401 entries.
AI.conclusion: Kumiho provides a formally grounded, graph-native cognitive memory architecture that unifies belief management and artifact versioning for AI agents. By aligning its property graph operations with AGM belief revision and Hansson’s postulates, it offers a rigorous semantic foundation. The combination of prospective indexing, event extraction, and LLM-based reranking leads to substantial gains on challenging cognitive memory benchmarks, setting new state-of-the-art results while remaining model-agnostic and cost-efficient. This suggests that architecturally sophisticated, formally motivated memory systems can significantly improve the reliability and capability of LLM-based agents, particularly on tasks requiring robust long-term and implicit constraint recall.

title: Tabular LLMs for Interpretable Few-Shot Alzheimer's Disease Prediction with Multimodal Biomedical Data
summary: Accurate diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease (AD) requires handling tabular biomarker data, yet such data are often small and incomplete, where deep learning models frequently fail to outperform classical methods. Pretrained large language models (LLMs) offer few-shot generalization, structured reasoning, and interpretable outputs, providing a powerful paradigm shift for clinical prediction. We propose TAP-GPT Tabular Alzheimer's Prediction GPT, a domain-adapted tabular LLM framework built on TableGPT2 and fine-tuned for few-shot AD classification using tabular prompts rather than plain texts. We evaluate TAP-GPT across four ADNI-derived datasets, including QT-PAD biomarkers and region-level structural MRI, amyloid PET, and tau PET for binary AD classification. Across multimodal and unimodal settings, TAP-GPT improves upon its backbone models and outperforms traditional machine learning baselines in the few-shot setting while remaining competitive with state-of-the-art general-purpose LLMs. We show that feature selection mitigates degradation in high-dimensional inputs and that TAP-GPT maintains stable performance under simulated and real-world missingness without imputation. Additionally, TAP-GPT produces structured, modality-aware reasoning aligned with established AD biology and shows greater stability under self-reflection, supporting its use in iterative multi-agent systems. To our knowledge, this is the first systematic application of a tabular-specialized LLM to multimodal biomarker-based AD prediction, demonstrating that such pretrained models can effectively address structured clinical prediction tasks and laying the foundation for tabular LLM-driven multi-agent clinical decision-support systems. The source code is publicly available on GitHub: https://github.com/sophie-kearney/TAP-GPT.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TAP-GPT, a tabular-specialized large language model adapted for few-shot prediction of Alzheimer’s disease from multimodal biomarker tables, showing improved or competitive performance versus classical ML and general LLMs, robustness to missing data, and interpretable, biologically plausible reasoning.
AI.motivation: Clinical prediction of Alzheimer’s disease relies on heterogeneous biomarker tables that are small, high-dimensional, and incomplete, making it difficult for standard deep learning to beat classical methods. There is a need for models that can generalize from few labeled examples, handle missingness without heavy preprocessing, integrate multiple modalities, and provide interpretable reasoning aligned with clinical knowledge. Recent LLMs show strong few-shot and reasoning abilities in text, suggesting they might be repurposed for tabular clinical data, but this potential has not been systematically explored for multimodal biomarker-based AD prediction.
AI.method: The authors build TAP-GPT, a domain-adapted tabular LLM for Alzheimer’s prediction, starting from TableGPT2 and fine-tuning it on AD-specific tabular prompts instead of free text. They encode multimodal biomarker data (QT-PAD biomarkers, regional MRI, amyloid PET, tau PET) into structured tabular prompts and perform few-shot binary AD classification. They evaluate TAP-GPT on four ADNI-derived datasets in both unimodal and multimodal configurations, compare against traditional ML baselines and backbone tabular models, and benchmark against state-of-the-art general-purpose LLMs. They further study the impact of feature selection for high-dimensional inputs, analyze robustness to simulated and real missing data without imputation, and qualitatively examine the model’s modality-aware reasoning and its stability under self-reflection for potential multi-agent use.
AI.result: Across four ADNI-based datasets, TAP-GPT consistently improves over its own backbone tabular models and outperforms classical ML baselines in few-shot scenarios, while delivering performance comparable to strong general-purpose LLMs. Feature selection reduces performance degradation with high-dimensional inputs, enabling more reliable modeling of regional imaging data. TAP-GPT maintains stable accuracy under both synthetic and naturally occurring missingness patterns even without explicit imputation strategies. The model’s generated reasoning is structured and modality-aware, aligning with known Alzheimer’s disease biology, and exhibits higher stability under self-reflective re-evaluation compared with alternatives, indicating suitability for iterative multi-agent clinical pipelines.
AI.conclusion: TAP-GPT demonstrates that a tabular-specialized, domain-adapted LLM can effectively tackle multimodal biomarker-based Alzheimer’s disease prediction in few-shot, small-sample, and missing-data settings, surpassing classical baselines and rivaling general LLMs while offering interpretable, biologically coherent reasoning. The work represents the first systematic application of such a tabular LLM to this task and suggests that pretrained tabular models are a promising foundation for future multi-agent, LLM-driven clinical decision-support systems operating on structured medical data. The public release of code supports reproducibility and further research in tabular LLMs for clinical applications.

title: Contrastive Reasoning Alignment: Reinforcement Learning from Hidden Representations
summary: We propose CRAFT, a red-teaming alignment framework that leverages model reasoning capabilities and hidden representations to improve robustness against jailbreak attacks. Unlike prior defenses that operate primarily at the output level, CRAFT aligns large reasoning models to generate safety-aware reasoning traces by explicitly optimizing objectives defined over the hidden state space. Methodologically, CRAFT integrates contrastive representation learning with reinforcement learning to separate safe and unsafe reasoning trajectories, yielding a latent-space geometry that supports robust, reasoning-level safety alignment. Theoretically, we show that incorporating latent-textual consistency into GRPO eliminates superficially aligned policies by ruling them out as local optima. Empirically, we evaluate CRAFT on multiple safety benchmarks using two strong reasoning models, Qwen3-4B-Thinking and R1-Distill-Llama-8B, where it consistently outperforms state-of-the-art defenses such as IPO and SafeKey. Notably, CRAFT delivers an average 79.0% improvement in reasoning safety and 87.7% improvement in final-response safety over the base models, demonstrating the effectiveness of hidden-space reasoning alignment.

AI.tldr: CRAFT is a red-teaming alignment framework that operates in the model’s hidden representation space to make reasoning models more robust to jailbreak attacks.
AI.motivation: Existing safety defenses mostly work at the output level and can be bypassed, especially for large reasoning models whose internal reasoning may remain unsafe even if final outputs look aligned. The authors aim to achieve deeper, reasoning-level safety by shaping the model’s internal representations to distinguish safe from unsafe reasoning trajectories.
AI.method: CRAFT trains models to produce safety-aware reasoning traces by optimizing objectives over hidden states. It uses contrastive representation learning to separate safe and unsafe reasoning paths in latent space, and combines this with reinforcement learning (specifically a GRPO-based method with a latent-textual consistency term) so that policies leading to unsafe or superficially aligned behaviors are discouraged and removed as local optima.
AI.result: On several safety benchmarks and two strong reasoning models (Qwen3-4B-Thinking and R1-Distill-Llama-8B), CRAFT consistently beats state-of-the-art defenses such as IPO and SafeKey. It yields large gains: about 79.0% improvement in reasoning safety and 87.7% improvement in final-response safety over unaligned base models.
AI.conclusion: Aligning models in hidden representation space—by separating safe and unsafe reasoning trajectories and enforcing latent-textual consistency—substantially improves robustness to jailbreak attacks. CRAFT shows that reasoning-level, latent-space alignment can outperform existing output-level defenses on strong reasoning models.

title: CODMAS: A Dialectic Multi-Agent Collaborative Framework for Structured RTL Optimization
summary: Optimizing Register Transfer Level (RTL) code is a critical step in Electronic Design Automation (EDA) for improving power, performance, and area (PPA). We present CODMAS (Collaborative Optimization via a Dialectic Multi-Agent System), a framework that combines structured dialectic reasoning with domain-aware code generation and deterministic evaluation to automate RTL optimization. At the core of CODMAS are two dialectic agents: the Articulator, inspired by rubber-duck debugging, which articulates stepwise transformation plans and exposes latent assumptions; and the Hypothesis Partner, which predicts outcomes and reconciles deviations between expected and actual behavior to guide targeted refinements. These agents direct a Domain-Specific Coding Agent (DCA) to generate architecture-aware Verilog edits and a Code Evaluation Agent (CEA) to verify syntax, functionality, and PPA metrics. We introduce RTLOPT, a benchmark of 120 Verilog triples (unoptimized, optimized, testbench) for pipelining and clock-gating transformations. Across proprietary and open LLMs, CODMAS achieves ~25% reduction in critical path delay for pipelining and ~22% power reduction for clock gating, while reducing functional and compilation failures compared to strong prompting and agentic baselines. These results demonstrate that structured multi-agent reasoning can significantly enhance automated RTL optimization and scale to more complex designs and broader optimization tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CODMAS, a structured multi-agent framework that uses dialectic reasoning and domain-aware agents to automatically optimize RTL Verilog code, improving power, performance, and area while reducing failures compared to strong prompting and existing agent baselines.
AI.motivation: RTL optimization is essential in EDA to improve power, performance, and area, but it is complex, labor-intensive, and error-prone when done manually or with naive LLM prompting. Existing LLM-based code optimization approaches lack structured reasoning, domain awareness, and reliable evaluation, leading to suboptimal transformations and frequent syntax/functional errors. The authors aim to create a more reliable, systematic way to use LLMs for RTL optimization that can robustly handle complex transformations like pipelining and clock gating and that can scale to broader design tasks.
AI.method: The authors design CODMAS, a dialectic multi-agent system for RTL optimization. Two main reasoning agents drive the process: (1) the Articulator, which, inspired by rubber-duck debugging, breaks down the optimization into explicit stepwise transformation plans and surfaces hidden assumptions; and (2) the Hypothesis Partner, which predicts the impact of proposed changes, compares expected vs. actual behavior, and guides corrective refinements.
