AI.tldr: A new multimodal, multilingual benchmark is proposed to evaluate how well models can detect and understand harmful versus safe dark humor across text, images, and videos in English and Arabic.
AI.motivation: Existing safety benchmarks focus mostly on explicit toxicity in text and miss the nuanced, context-dependent nature of dark humor, especially in multimedia and non-English settings. There is a need for a culturally grounded, reasoning-intensive benchmark that distinguishes safe from harmful jokes and tests models’ ability to handle implicit offensive content.
AI.method: The authors manually curate a dataset containing 3,000 texts and 6,000 images in English and Arabic, plus 1,200 videos covering English, Arabic, and language-independent content. They design strict annotation guidelines that label each item as Safe or Harmful; harmful items are further divided into Explicit (overt) and Implicit (covert) categories. They then systematically benchmark state-of-the-art open- and closed-source models across all three modalities.
AI.result: Closed-source models substantially outperform open-source models on this benchmark. Performance is also uneven across languages: models perform noticeably better on English than on Arabic, for both open- and closed-source systems. The benchmark exposes particular weaknesses in handling implicit (covert) harmful humor and culturally nuanced content.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal, multilingual dark humor requires deeper contextual and cultural reasoning than current models and benchmarks typically support. The new benchmark highlights gaps in existing systems—especially for Arabic and implicit harmful humor—and demonstrates the importance of culturally grounded, reasoning-aware safety alignment for future model development.

title: CoVerRL: Breaking the Consensus Trap in Label-Free Reasoning via Generator-Verifier Co-Evolution
summary: Label-free reinforcement learning enables large language models to improve reasoning capabilities without ground-truth supervision, typically by treating majority-voted answers as pseudo-labels. However, we identify a critical failure mode: as training maximizes self-consistency, output diversity collapses, causing the model to confidently reinforce systematic errors that evade detection. We term this the consensus trap. To escape it, we propose CoVerRL, a framework where a single model alternates between generator and verifier roles, with each capability bootstrapping the other. Majority voting provides noisy but informative supervision for training the verifier, while the improving verifier progressively filters self-consistent errors from pseudo-labels. This co-evolution creates a virtuous cycle that maintains high reward accuracy throughout training. Experiments across Qwen and Llama model families demonstrate that CoVerRL outperforms label-free baselines by 4.7-5.9\% on mathematical reasoning benchmarks. Moreover, self-verification accuracy improves from around 55\% to over 85\%, confirming that both capabilities genuinely co-evolve.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a failure mode in label-free RL for LLM reasoning, called the consensus trap, and proposes CoVerRL, a generator–verifier co-evolution framework that maintains diversity and improves both reasoning and self-verification accuracy.
AI.motivation: Label-free reinforcement learning lets LLMs improve reasoning without ground-truth labels by using majority-voted answers as pseudo-labels. However, the authors observe that as models optimize for self-consistency, their outputs become less diverse and can converge on confident but systematically wrong answers that majority voting cannot catch. This undermines the reliability of label-free training and limits its usefulness for scaling reasoning capabilities without expensive supervision. The work is motivated by the need to avoid this consensus trap and to safely extract more signal from noisy, self-generated data.
AI.method: The authors propose CoVerRL, where a single LLM alternates between a generator role (producing candidate solutions) and a verifier role (evaluating those solutions). Majority voting over generated answers is used as a noisy training signal to bootstrap the verifier. As the verifier improves, it is used to filter out self-consistent but incorrect answers from the pseudo-labels used to train the generator under reinforcement learning. This creates a co-evolution loop: the generator’s reasoning improves under filtered rewards, while the verifier’s judgment improves from training on increasingly accurate majority-vote data. The overall RL procedure optimizes for both high-quality reasoning and reliable self-verification without external labels.
AI.result: On mathematical reasoning benchmarks and across Qwen and Llama model families, CoVerRL achieves 4.7–5.9% higher performance than existing label-free RL baselines. In addition, the model’s self-verification accuracy—its ability to correctly judge its own outputs—rises from roughly 55% to over 85%, indicating strong gains not only in solution accuracy but also in internal evaluation capability.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that naive label-free RL is vulnerable to a consensus trap where self-consistency training collapses diversity and entrenches systematic errors. CoVerRL’s generator–verifier co-evolution provides a practical remedy, preserving reward accuracy and improving both reasoning and self-verification in a label-free setting. This demonstrates that carefully structured self-training can safely and effectively enhance LLM reasoning without requiring ground-truth supervision.

title: Process Supervision for Chain-of-Thought Reasoning via Monte Carlo Net Information Gain
summary: Multi-step reasoning improves the capabilities of large language models (LLMs) but increases the risk of errors propagating through intermediate steps. Process reward models (PRMs) mitigate this by scoring each step individually, enabling fine-grained supervision and improved reliability. Existing methods for training PRMs rely on costly human annotations or computationally intensive automatic labeling. We propose a novel approach to automatically generate step-level labels using Information Theory. Our method estimates how each reasoning step affects the likelihood of the correct answer, providing a signal of step quality. Importantly, it reduces computational complexity to $\mathcal{O}(N)$, improving over the previous $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$ methods. We demonstrate that these labels enable effective chain-of-thought selection in best-of-$K$ evaluation settings across diverse reasoning benchmarks, including mathematics, Python programming, SQL, and scientific question answering. This work enables scalable and efficient supervision of LLM reasoning, particularly for tasks where error propagation is critical.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an information-theoretic way to automatically label each step in multi-step reasoning traces for training process reward models, doing so more efficiently and enabling better selection of high-quality chain-of-thought reasoning in large language models.
AI.motivation: Multi-step reasoning makes LLMs more capable but also more error-prone, since early mistakes can propagate. Process reward models help by evaluating each intermediate step, but current PRM training depends on expensive human step-level annotation or heavy automatic procedures. There is a need for a scalable, low-cost, and reliable way to assign quality scores to each reasoning step so that LLM reasoning can be supervised and controlled at scale, especially in domains where error propagation has large impact.
AI.method: The authors introduce an information-theoretic labeling scheme that estimates, for each reasoning step in a chain-of-thought, how much that step changes the probability of the correct final answer. This change in likelihood serves as a quality signal for the step. The method is designed so that computing labels for an N-step reasoning trace requires only O(N) model evaluations, improving over prior O(N log N) automatic labeling methods. These step-level labels are then used to train or guide process reward models and to perform chain-of-thought selection in best-of-K settings.
AI.result: Using these information-theoretic step labels, the authors show improved chain-of-thought selection performance in best-of-K evaluation across several reasoning benchmarks. These benchmarks span multiple domains, including math problem solving, Python coding, SQL query generation, and scientific question answering, indicating that the learned step-level supervision generalizes across task types. The approach maintains or improves reliability while significantly reducing the computational cost of obtaining training labels compared to previous automatic labeling schemes.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that information-theoretic, automatically generated step-level labels provide an effective and scalable signal for supervising multi-step reasoning in LLMs. By reducing labeling complexity from O(N log N) to O(N) and demonstrating strong performance on diverse reasoning tasks, the approach makes process reward modeling more practical and efficient, particularly in scenarios where mitigating error propagation in long reasoning chains is crucial.

title: Text-to-Stage: Spatial Layouts from Long-form Narratives
summary: In this work, we probe the ability of a language model to demonstrate spatial reasoning from unstructured text, mimicking human capabilities and automating a process that benefits many downstream media applications. Concretely, we study the narrative-to-play task: inferring stage-play layouts (scenes, speaker positions, movements, and room types) from text that lacks explicit spatial, positional, or relational cues. We then introduce a dramaturgy-inspired deterministic evaluation suite and, finally, a training and inference recipe that combines rejection SFT using Best-of-N sampling with RL from verifiable rewards via GRPO. Experiments on a text-only corpus of classical English literature demonstrate improvements over vanilla models across multiple metrics (character attribution, spatial plausibility, and movement economy), as well as alignment with an LLM-as-a-judge and subjective human preferences.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how language models can infer stage-play spatial layouts (scenes, positions, movements, room types) from narrative text with minimal explicit spatial cues, and proposes a specialized evaluation suite plus a training recipe combining rejection SFT and RL with verifiable rewards, showing improved spatial reasoning and character attribution on classical literature.
AI.motivation: Many narratives describe events and dialogue without explicit spatial annotations, yet humans can infer rich spatial layouts that are useful for media applications like staging, visualization, and adaptation. Existing language models struggle with robust spatial reasoning from such unstructured text, and there is a lack of task formulations and evaluation frameworks focused on this ability. The authors aim to formalize and measure this capability and to improve LMs so they can automatically reconstruct stage-play–like layouts from narrative text.
AI.method: The authors define the narrative-to-play task, where a model must infer scenes, speaker positions, movements, and room types from narrative input that has few explicit spatial or relational cues. They design a dramaturgy-inspired deterministic evaluation suite targeting aspects like character attribution, spatial plausibility, and movement economy. For training, they use a two-stage recipe: (1) rejection supervised fine-tuning with Best-of-N sampling to select better outputs, and (2) reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards via GRPO to further optimize behavior according to their spatial and dramaturgical metrics. They evaluate on a text-only corpus of classical English literature using both automatic metrics and LLM-as-a-judge plus human preference assessments.
AI.result: Models trained with the proposed recipe outperform vanilla baselines on multiple dimensions, including more accurate character attribution, higher spatial plausibility of inferred layouts, and more economical character movements. Their outputs also align better with judgments from a separate LLM and with subjective human preferences, indicating that the approach yields more coherent and theatrically sensible spatial reconstructions from narrative text.
AI.conclusion: The study shows that language models can be steered to perform nontrivial spatial reasoning from unstructured text in the narrative-to-play setting. A dramaturgy-grounded deterministic evaluation framework, combined with rejection SFT and RL from verifiable rewards, leads to measurable gains over standard models. This suggests a promising direction for automating spatial layout inference for narrative media applications and highlights the value of task-specific structural objectives and evaluation schemes for improving LMs’ reasoning capabilities.

title: Event-Centric Human Value Understanding in News-Domain Texts: An Actor-Conditioned, Multi-Granularity Benchmark
summary: Existing human value datasets do not directly support value understanding in factual news: many are actor-agnostic, rely on isolated utterances or synthetic scenarios, and lack explicit event structure or value direction. We present \textbf{NEVU} (\textbf{N}ews \textbf{E}vent-centric \textbf{V}alue \textbf{U}nderstanding), a benchmark for \emph{actor-conditioned}, \emph{event-centric}, and \emph{direction-aware} human value recognition in factual news. NEVU evaluates whether models can identify value cues, attribute them to the correct actor, and determine value direction from grounded evidence. Built from 2{,}865 English news articles, NEVU organizes annotations at four semantic unit levels (\textbf{Subevent}, \textbf{behavior-based composite event}, \textbf{story-based composite event}, and \textbf{Article}) and labels \mbox{(unit, actor)} pairs for fine-grained evaluation across local and composite contexts. The annotations are produced through an LLM-assisted pipeline with staged verification and targeted human auditing. Using a hierarchical value space with \textbf{54} fine-grained values and \textbf{20} coarse-grained categories, NEVU covers 45{,}793 unit--actor pairs and 168{,}061 directed value instances. We provide unified baselines for proprietary and open-source LLMs, and find that lightweight adaptation (LoRA) consistently improves open-source models, showing that although NEVU is designed primarily as a benchmark, it also supports supervised adaptation beyond prompting-only evaluation. Data availability is described in Appendix~\ref{app:data_code_availability}.

AI.tldr: They introduce NEVU, a new benchmark dataset for understanding human values in factual news, focusing on who holds which values, in which events, and in what direction (supported or opposed).
AI.motivation: Existing human value datasets are not well suited for factual news understanding because they usually ignore who the value holder is, use decontextualized or synthetic text, and lack explicit event structure and value direction (support vs opposition). This limits the ability to evaluate models on realistic, complex news where multiple actors and events interact.
AI.method: They build NEVU from 2,865 English news articles using an LLM-assisted annotation pipeline with multiple stages of verification and selective human auditing. Annotations are organized into four nested semantic unit levels (subevents, behavior-based composite events, story-based composite events, and whole articles). For each (unit, actor) pair, they label which values are expressed and in what direction, using a hierarchical value taxonomy of 54 fine-grained values grouped into 20 coarse categories. They then benchmark various proprietary and open-source LLMs and apply lightweight fine-tuning (LoRA) to open-source models.
AI.result: NEVU consists of 45,793 unit–actor pairs and 168,061 directed value instances, providing dense, structured supervision over news articles. Baseline experiments show that open-source LLMs benefit consistently from lightweight adaptation (LoRA) on NEVU compared with prompt-only use, while proprietary models provide additional reference performance levels.
AI.conclusion: NEVU offers a new, event-centric and actor-aware benchmark for value understanding in factual news, capturing both value attribution and direction across multiple levels of narrative structure. Beyond serving as an evaluation benchmark, it can also support supervised adaptation of open-source language models, as shown by performance gains with LoRA fine-tuning. Data and code access details are provided separately in the appendix.

title: How do LLMs Compute Verbal Confidence
summary: Verbal confidence -- prompting LLMs to state their confidence as a number or category -- is widely used to extract uncertainty estimates from black-box models. However, how LLMs internally generate such scores remains unknown. We address two questions: first, when confidence is computed - just-in-time when requested, or automatically during answer generation and cached for later retrieval; and second, what verbal confidence represents - token log-probabilities, or a richer evaluation of answer quality? Focusing on Gemma 3 27B and Qwen 2.5 7B, we provide convergent evidence for cached retrieval. Activation steering, patching, noising, and swap experiments reveal that confidence representations emerge at answer-adjacent positions before appearing at the verbalization site. Attention blocking pinpoints the information flow: confidence is gathered from answer tokens, cached at the first post-answer position, then retrieved for output. Critically, linear probing and variance partitioning reveal that these cached representations explain substantial variance in verbal confidence beyond token log-probabilities, suggesting a richer answer-quality evaluation rather than a simple fluency readout. These findings demonstrate that verbal confidence reflects automatic, sophisticated self-evaluation -- not post-hoc reconstruction -- with implications for understanding metacognition in LLMs and improving calibration.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how large language models internally generate verbal confidence scores and finds that they pre-compute and cache rich, answer-quality representations that are later retrieved for explicit confidence reports, going beyond simple token probability readouts.
AI.motivation: Verbal confidence is widely used as a proxy for uncertainty in black-box LLMs, but it is unclear whether these scores reflect on-the-fly guesses based on token probabilities or deeper internal assessments formed during answer generation. Understanding the mechanism and content of these confidence signals is crucial for interpreting model metacognition and improving calibration and safety.
AI.method: The authors study two specific LLMs (Gemma 3 27B and Qwen 2.5 7B) and use a suite of mechanistic interpretability tools: activation steering, activation patching, adding noise to activations, and swapping activations across contexts to track where and when confidence information arises. They also use attention blocking to trace information flow from answer tokens to a post-answer cache position and back to the confidence token. Finally, they apply linear probing and variance partitioning to quantify how much of verbal confidence can be explained by these internal cached representations versus simple token log-probabilities.
AI.result: The experiments show that confidence-related representations appear in the network at positions adjacent to the answer tokens before the explicit confidence token is produced. Manipulating these activations changes the reported confidence without changing the answer, indicating that the model caches confidence information. Attention analyses reveal a flow where the model aggregates information from answer tokens, stores it at the first position after the answer, and later reads it out when asked for confidence. Probing analyses indicate that these cached representations explain substantial variance in the eventual confidence output beyond what is captured by token log-probabilities.
AI.conclusion: Verbal confidence in the studied LLMs is not a post-hoc construction based solely on surface fluency or token log-probabilities. Instead, the models automatically compute and cache rich internal assessments of answer quality during generation, then later retrieve these for explicit confidence reporting. This suggests that LLMs possess a form of built-in metacognitive processing and that leveraging these internal representations could lead to better-calibrated and more reliable uncertainty estimates.

title: Mitigating LLM Hallucinations through Domain-Grounded Tiered Retrieval
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved unprecedented fluency but remain susceptible to "hallucinations" - the generation of factually incorrect or ungrounded content. This limitation is particularly critical in high-stakes domains where reliability is paramount. We propose a domain-grounded tiered retrieval and verification architecture designed to systematically intercept factual inaccuracies by shifting LLMs from stochastic pattern-matchers to verified truth-seekers. The proposed framework utilizes a four-phase, self-regulating pipeline implemented via LangGraph: (I) Intrinsic Verification with Early-Exit logic to optimize compute, (II) Adaptive Search Routing utilizing a Domain Detector to target subject-specific archives, (III) Corrective Document Grading (CRAG) to filter irrelevant context, and (IV) Extrinsic Regeneration followed by atomic claim-level verification. The system was evaluated across 650 queries from five diverse benchmarks: TimeQA v2, FreshQA v2, HaluEval General, MMLU Global Facts, and TruthfulQA. Empirical results demonstrate that the pipeline consistently outperforms zero-shot baselines across all environments. Win rates peaked at 83.7% in TimeQA v2 and 78.0% in MMLU Global Facts, confirming high efficacy in domains requiring granular temporal and numerical precision. Groundedness scores remained robustly stable between 78.8% and 86.4% across factual-answer rows. While the architecture provides a robust fail-safe for misinformation, a persistent failure mode of "False-Premise Overclaiming" was identified. These findings provide a detailed empirical characterization of multi-stage RAG behavior and suggest that future work should prioritize pre-retrieval "answerability" nodes to further bridge the reliability gap in conversational AI.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a multi-stage, domain-grounded retrieval and verification pipeline to reduce hallucinations in large language models, showing strong empirical gains in factual accuracy and groundedness across several benchmarks.
AI.motivation: LLMs are highly fluent but prone to hallucinations, which is especially problematic in high‑stakes, fact-sensitive domains. Existing approaches like basic RAG or zero-shot prompting are insufficiently reliable, particularly for time-sensitive, numerically precise, or domain-specific queries. The authors aim to systematically transform LLM behavior from unconstrained text generation into a process tightly constrained by verified external knowledge, providing a more dependable safeguard against misinformation.
AI.method: The authors design a four-phase, self-regulating architecture implemented with LangGraph. (I) Intrinsic Verification: The LLM’s initial answer is checked using internal consistency and uncertainty signals, with an Early-Exit mechanism to save computation when confidence is high. (II) Adaptive Search Routing: A Domain Detector classifies the query’s subject area and routes it to appropriate, specialized document collections for retrieval. (III) Corrective Document Grading (CRAG): Retrieved documents are scored and filtered to remove irrelevant or low-quality context, keeping only the most supportive evidence. (IV) Extrinsic Regeneration & Claim Verification: The answer is regenerated conditioned on the curated evidence, and then broken down into atomic factual claims, each of which is independently checked against the retrieved sources. This creates a multi-stage feedback loop around the LLM to catch and correct hallucinations.
AI.result: Evaluated on 650 queries spanning five benchmarks (TimeQA v2, FreshQA v2, HaluEval General, MMLU Global Facts, TruthfulQA), the pipeline consistently beats zero-shot LLM baselines. Win rates reach up to 83.7% on TimeQA v2 and 78.0% on MMLU Global Facts, indicating strong gains in temporally and numerically precise tasks. Groundedness of answers stays high and stable, between 78.8% and 86.4% across factual responses. However, a notable remaining failure pattern—“False-Premise Overclaiming”—shows that the system can still confidently answer questions whose underlying premises are incorrect, revealing limits of the current design.
AI.conclusion: A structured, domain-aware, multi-stage retrieval-and-verification pipeline can significantly improve the factual reliability and groundedness of LLM outputs across diverse benchmarks. The architecture acts as a robust fail-safe against many forms of hallucination but does not fully eliminate errors, particularly when questions themselves encode false premises. The authors argue that future architectures should introduce explicit pre-retrieval “answerability” checks or nodes to detect unanswerable or ill-posed questions, further closing the reliability gap for conversational AI in critical applications.

title: DebugLM: Learning Traceable Training Data Provenance for LLMs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are trained through multi-stage pipelines over heterogeneous data sources, yet developers lack a principled way to pinpoint the specific data responsible for an observed behavior. This lack of observability reduces debugging to reactive patching and makes failures prone to recur under distribution shift or subsequent model updates. To address this limitation, we propose DebugLM, a framework that equips LLMs with built-in data provenance, enabling them to explicitly trace the origins of their behaviors to specific training data sources. Specifically, the model learns to associate its responses with unique provenance tags that indicate the responsible dataset, empowering developers to precisely identify where undesirable behaviors are learned. Building on this capability, DebugLM further supports targeted test-time remediation, enabling developers to selectively trigger targeted refusal for specified data sources without retraining or modifying model parameters. Experiments demonstrate that DebugLM provides accurate behavior tracing in multi-stage training pipelines and effective test-time remediation while preserving the general utility of the model.

AI.tldr: DebugLM adds built-in data provenance to LLMs so that specific training data sources responsible for behaviors can be identified and selectively mitigated at test time.
AI.motivation: LLM training uses complex multi-stage pipelines and heterogeneous datasets, but developers currently cannot see which training data sources cause particular model behaviors. This lack of observability forces ad hoc fixes and makes it hard to reliably prevent recurring failures, especially under distribution shifts or model updates.
AI.method: Introduce DebugLM, a framework where the LLM is trained to associate its responses with unique provenance tags representing underlying training data sources. The model learns to output or internally use these tags to trace behaviors back to data sources. On top of this, DebugLM supports mechanisms at inference time to condition the model on refusing behaviors linked to specified data sources, without changing parameters or retraining.
AI.result: Experiments on multi-stage training pipelines show that DebugLM can accurately trace which datasets drive specific model behaviors and that the test-time refusal mechanism can effectively suppress behaviors tied to unwanted data sources. These interventions maintain the overall utility and performance of the LLM.
AI.conclusion: Equipping LLMs with explicit data provenance via DebugLM makes model behavior more observable and controllable. Developers can identify which datasets lead to undesirable behaviors and apply precise, test-time remediation—such as targeted refusals—without retraining, while preserving general model usefulness.

title: Pretrained Multilingual Transformers Reveal Quantitative Distance Between Human Languages
summary: Understanding the distance between human languages is central to linguistics, anthropology, and tracing human evolutionary history. Yet, while linguistics has long provided rich qualitative accounts of cross-linguistic variation, a unified and scalable quantitative approach to measuring language distance remains lacking. In this paper, we introduce a method that leverages pretrained multilingual language models as systematic instruments for linguistic measurement. Specifically, we show that the spontaneously emerged attention mechanisms of these models provide a robust, tokenization-agnostic measure of cross-linguistic distance, termed Attention Transport Distance (ATD). By treating attention matrices as probability distributions and measuring their geometric divergence via optimal transport, we quantify the representational distance between languages during translation. Applying ATD to a large and diverse set of languages, we demonstrate that the resulting distances recover established linguistic groupings with high fidelity and reveal patterns aligned with geographic and contact-induced relationships. Furthermore, incorporating ATD as a regularizer improves transfer performance in low-resource machine translation. Our results establish a principled foundation for testing linguistic hypotheses using artificial neural networks. This framework transforms multilingual models into powerful tools for quantitative linguistic discovery, facilitating more equitable multilingual AI.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Attention Transport Distance (ATD), a quantitative, model-based measure of distance between human languages derived from multilingual transformer attention patterns.
AI.motivation: Quantitatively measuring distances between human languages is crucial for linguistics, anthropology, and understanding human evolution, but current approaches are mostly qualitative or fragmented and do not scale well. With the rise of powerful multilingual language models, there is an opportunity to repurpose their internal representations as systematic, unified instruments for measuring cross-linguistic relationships in a data-driven way.
AI.method: The authors use pretrained multilingual language models and interpret their attention matrices as probability distributions over token alignments during translation. They then apply optimal transport theory to compute a geometric divergence between these attention distributions across languages, defining this measure as Attention Transport Distance (ATD). ATD is designed to be tokenization-agnostic, relying on attention patterns rather than specific vocabulary units. They compute ATD across many language pairs and layers/heads, then analyze how the induced distance matrix relates to known language families, geography, and contact patterns. They also integrate ATD as a regularization term in training low-resource machine translation systems to encourage better cross-lingual transfer.
AI.result: ATD, computed from multilingual model attention, recovers known linguistic groupings with high fidelity, clustering languages according to established families and subgroups. It further reveals distance patterns that align with geographic proximity and contact-induced relationships, indicating that the model’s internal attention structure encodes meaningful cross-linguistic information. When used as a regularizer in low-resource machine translation, ATD improves transfer performance, showing that this linguistic distance measure is not only descriptive but also practically beneficial for multilingual NLP.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that attention mechanisms in multilingual language models can serve as robust quantitative instruments for measuring linguistic distance, yielding the Attention Transport Distance metric. ATD provides a scalable, tokenization-agnostic way to uncover both genealogical and contact-based relations among languages, and it can enhance low-resource machine translation through regularization. Overall, the work establishes a principled framework for using neural models to test linguistic hypotheses and promotes more equitable multilingual AI by enabling data-driven linguistic discovery across many languages.

title: IndicSafe: A Benchmark for Evaluating Multilingual LLM Safety in South Asia
summary:
As large language models (LLMs) are deployed in multilingual settings, their safety behavior in culturally diverse, low-resource languages remains poorly understood. We present the first systematic evaluation of LLM safety across 12 Indic languages, spoken by over 1.2 billion people but underrepresented in LLM training data. Using a dataset of 6,000 culturally grounded prompts spanning caste, religion, gender, health, and politics, we assess 10 leading LLMs on translated variants of the prompt.
  Our analysis reveals significant safety drift: cross-language agreement is just 12.8\%, and \texttt{SAFE} rate variance exceeds 17\% across languages. Some models over-refuse benign prompts in low-resource scripts, overflag politically sensitive topics, while others fail to flag unsafe generations. We quantify these failures using prompt-level entropy, category bias scores, and multilingual consistency indices.
  Our findings highlight critical safety generalization gaps in multilingual LLMs and show that safety alignment does not transfer evenly across languages. We release \textsc{IndicSafe}, the first benchmark to enable culturally informed safety evaluation for Indic deployments, and advocate for language-aware alignment strategies grounded in regional harms.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how safely large language models behave across 12 Indic languages and finds large inconsistencies and failures, introducing a new benchmark (IndicSafe) to study and improve multilingual safety alignment.
AI.motivation: Current LLM safety work is mostly in high-resource, often English-centric contexts, leaving little understanding of how safety mechanisms behave in low-resource, culturally diverse Indic languages used by over a billion people. As these models get deployed in such regions, it is crucial to know whether safety policies and alignments transfer reliably across languages and scripts, especially on culturally sensitive topics.
AI.method: The authors build a 6,000-prompt dataset, IndicSafe, covering culturally grounded and sensitive domains such as caste, religion, gender, health, and politics. They translate prompts into 12 Indic languages and evaluate 10 leading LLMs on all variants. They then measure cross-language consistency and safety behavior using metrics like cross-language agreement, SAFE-rate variance, prompt-level entropy, category bias scores, and multilingual consistency indices.
AI.result: They find major safety drift across languages: only 12.8% agreement in safety decisions across language variants of the same prompt and more than 17% variance in SAFE rates. Some models are overly conservative in low-resource scripts and on political prompts, refusing benign content, while others fail to detect unsafe generations, leading to both over- and under-enforcement of safety policies depending on language and topic.
AI.conclusion: Safety alignment learned in high-resource languages does not transfer uniformly to Indic languages, resulting in inconsistent and sometimes harmful safety behavior. The IndicSafe benchmark enables systematic, culturally grounded safety evaluation for Indic deployments and underscores the need for language-aware, region-specific alignment strategies that are sensitive to local harms and contexts.

title: Efficient Training-Free Multi-Token Prediction via Embedding-Space Probing
summary: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit latent multi-token prediction (MTP) capabilities despite being trained solely for next-token generation. We propose a simple, training-free MTP approach that probes an LLM using on-the-fly mask tokens drawn from its embedding space, enabling parallel prediction of future tokens without modifying model weights or relying on auxiliary draft models. Our method constructs a speculative token tree by sampling top-K candidates from mask-token logits and applies a lightweight pruning strategy to retain high-probability continuations. During decoding, candidate predictions are verified in parallel, resulting in lossless generation while substantially reducing the number of model calls and improving token throughput. Across benchmarks, our probing-based MTP consistently outperforms existing training-free baselines, increasing acceptance length by approximately 12\% on LLaMA3 and 8--12\% on Qwen3, and achieving throughput gains of up to 15--19\%. Finally, we provide theoretical insights and empirical evidence showing that decoder layers naturally align mask-token representations with next-token states, enabling accurate multi-step prediction without retraining or auxiliary models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a training-free method to make large language models predict multiple future tokens in parallel using mask-like probes in the embedding space, building a speculative token tree that is verified losslessly during decoding to speed up generation.
AI.motivation: LLMs are usually trained only for single-step next-token prediction, but they empirically show some ability to anticipate multiple future tokens. Existing multi-token prediction and speculative decoding methods often require retraining, auxiliary draft models, or architectural changes. There is a need for a simple, training-free way to harness latent multi-token prediction capabilities to reduce computation and improve throughput without sacrificing generation quality.
AI.method: The authors introduce a probing-based multi-token prediction (MTP) scheme. They inject on-the-fly mask tokens drawn from the LLM’s embedding space into the decoding process to elicit predictions of several future tokens in parallel. From the logits of these mask positions, they sample top-K candidate tokens and form a speculative token tree that represents possible continuations. A lightweight pruning strategy keeps only high-probability branches. During actual decoding, the model verifies these speculative sequences in parallel; accepted tokens are committed, while rejected branches are discarded. This operates without changing model weights, without extra draft models, and remains fully compatible with standard autoregressive decoding.
AI.result: On benchmarks with models like LLaMA3 and Qwen3, the method improves multi-token acceptance length by about 12% for LLaMA3 and 8–12% for Qwen3 compared to prior training-free baselines. These gains translate into token throughput improvements of roughly 15–19%, all while maintaining lossless generation quality (no degradation relative to standard decoding).
AI.conclusion: The work shows that decoder layers in standard LLMs already structure mask-token representations to align with next-token states, enabling accurate multi-step prediction without retraining or auxiliary models. By probing this emergent structure with embedding-space masks and speculative token trees, one can achieve efficient, lossless multi-token generation and surpass existing training-free MTP approaches in both acceptance length and throughput.

title: ShapleyLaw: A Game-Theoretic Approach to Multilingual Scaling Laws
summary: In multilingual pretraining, the test loss of a pretrained model is heavily influenced by the proportion of each language in the pretraining data, namely the \textit{language mixture ratios}. Multilingual scaling laws can predict the test loss under different language mixture ratios and can therefore be used to estimate the optimal ratios. However, the current approaches to multilingual scaling laws do not measure the \textit{cross-lingual transfer} effect, resulting in suboptimal mixture ratios. In this paper, we consider multilingual pretraining as a cooperative game in which each language acts as a player that jointly contributes to pretraining, gaining the resulting reduction in test loss as the payoff. Consequently, from the perspective of cooperative game theory, we quantify the cross-lingual transfer from each language by its contribution in the game, and propose a game-theoretic multilingual scaling law called \textit{ShapleyLaw}. Our experiments show that ShapleyLaw outperforms baseline methods in model performance prediction and language mixture optimization.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ShapleyLaw, a game-theoretic multilingual scaling law that models languages as cooperative game players and uses their Shapley-value-like contributions to better predict test loss and optimize language mixture ratios in multilingual pretraining.
AI.motivation: In multilingual pretraining, model performance (test loss) strongly depends on how much data from each language is used. Existing multilingual scaling laws can extrapolate performance for different language mixtures but fail to explicitly capture cross-lingual transfer between languages, leading to suboptimal data allocation across languages. The authors aim to develop a framework that directly accounts for how training on one language benefits others, enabling more accurate loss prediction and better choice of language mixture ratios.
AI.method: The authors frame multilingual pretraining as a cooperative game where each language is a player. The joint reduction in test loss produced by training on a mixture of languages is treated as the total payoff of the coalition. Using cooperative game theory, they quantify each language’s marginal contribution (akin to a Shapley value) to this payoff, which captures its cross-lingual transfer effect. They then embed these contributions into a new multilingual scaling law, ShapleyLaw, that predicts test loss as a function of language mixture ratios while explicitly modeling inter-language transfer. This law is then used to optimize the choice of mixture ratios.
AI.result: Empirically, ShapleyLaw yields more accurate test-loss predictions than baseline multilingual scaling laws that do not explicitly model cross-lingual transfer. When used to choose language mixture ratios for pretraining, the resulting mixtures yield better downstream model performance than those derived from existing methods.
AI.conclusion: Modeling multilingual pretraining as a cooperative game and using game-theoretic contributions of languages allows explicit quantification of cross-lingual transfer. The proposed ShapleyLaw scaling framework improves both performance prediction and the optimization of language mixture ratios, indicating that game-theoretic treatment of languages is a promising direction for multilingual pretraining design.

title: Gender Disambiguation in Machine Translation: Diagnostic Evaluation in Decoder-Only Architectures
summary: While Large Language Models achieve state-of-the-art results across a wide range of NLP tasks, they remain prone to systematic biases. Among these, gender bias is particularly salient in MT, due to systematic differences across languages in whether and how gender is marked. As a result, translation often requires disambiguating implicit source signals into explicit gender-marked forms. In this context, standard benchmarks may capture broad disparities but fail to reflect the full complexity of gender bias in modern MT. In this paper, we extend recent frameworks on bias evaluation by: (i) introducing a novel measure coined "Prior Bias", capturing a model's default gender assumptions, and (ii) applying the framework to decoder-only MT models. Our results show that, despite their scale and state-of-the-art status, decoder-only models do not generally outperform encoder-decoder architectures on gender-specific metrics; however, post-training (e.g., instruction tuning) not only improves contextual awareness but also reduces the masculine Prior Bias.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new way to measure and analyze gender bias in machine translation, focusing on large decoder-only language models.
AI.motivation: Large Language Models, particularly in machine translation, still exhibit systematic gender bias, partly because many target languages require explicit gender marking that is not present in the source. Existing benchmarks show broad disparities but miss finer-grained aspects of how gender bias appears in modern MT systems. There is a need for better metrics and for understanding how new model architectures (decoder-only LLMs) behave with respect to gender bias.
AI.method: The authors extend recent bias-evaluation frameworks for MT in two ways: (i) they define a new metric called "Prior Bias" that measures a model’s default gender assumptions in translation, i.e., how it chooses gendered forms when the source is ambiguous, and (ii) they adapt and apply this extended framework specifically to decoder-only MT models (large LLMs used for translation). They compare these models to traditional encoder-decoder MT systems and study the effect of post-training techniques such as instruction tuning.
AI.result: Decoder-only models, despite being large and state-of-the-art, generally do not outperform traditional encoder-decoder models on gender-specific evaluation metrics. The analysis using the Prior Bias metric reveals a masculine-skewed default in these models. However, models that undergo post-training, such as instruction tuning, show better use of contextual cues and a reduction in masculine Prior Bias.
AI.conclusion: State-of-the-art decoder-only LLMs are not inherently better at handling gender in MT than encoder-decoder architectures and maintain a masculine default bias. The proposed Prior Bias metric provides a more nuanced view of gender bias in MT. Post-training techniques like instruction tuning can meaningfully improve contextual gender handling and mitigate default masculine bias, highlighting the importance of specialized training for fairness in MT.

title: ConGA: Guidelines for Contextual Gender Annotation. A Framework for Annotating Gender in Machine Translation
summary: Handling gender across languages remains a persistent challenge for Machine Translation (MT) and Large Language Models (LLMs), especially when translating from gender-neutral languages into morphologically gendered ones, such as English to Italian. English largely omits grammatical gender, while Italian requires explicit agreement across multiple grammatical categories. This asymmetry often leads MT systems to default to masculine forms, reinforcing bias and reducing translation accuracy. To address this issue, we present the Contextual Gender Annotation (ConGA) framework, a linguistically grounded set of guidelines for word-level gender annotation. The scheme distinguishes between semantic gender in English through three tags, Masculine (M), Feminine (F), and Ambiguous (A), and grammatical gender realisation in Italian (Masculine (M), Feminine (F)), combined with entity-level identifiers for cross-sentence tracking. We apply ConGA to the gENder-IT dataset, creating a gold-standard resource for evaluating gender bias in translation. Our results reveal systematic masculine overuse and inconsistent feminine realisation, highlighting persistent limitations of current MT systems. By combining fine-grained linguistic annotation with quantitative evaluation, this work offers both a methodology and a benchmark for building more gender-aware and multilingual NLP systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ConGA, a linguistically grounded framework for annotating and evaluating how machine translation systems handle gender when translating from gender-neutral English to gendered Italian, revealing systematic masculine bias and offering a benchmark for more gender-aware NLP.
AI.motivation: Machine Translation and LLMs struggle with accurately expressing gender when translating from largely gender-neutral languages like English into morphologically gendered ones like Italian. This mismatch leads systems to overuse masculine forms, which both encodes bias and reduces translation quality. Existing evaluations are often coarse-grained and lack a detailed, linguistically principled way to track how gender is represented at the word and discourse level, particularly across sentences for the same entity. The paper aims to fill this gap by providing a fine-grained annotation scheme and a high-quality dataset to diagnose and quantify gender bias in translation.
AI.method: The authors design the Contextual Gender Annotation (ConGA) framework, a word-level gender annotation scheme grounded in linguistic analysis. In English, they distinguish semantic gender using three labels: Masculine (M), Feminine (F), and Ambiguous (A). In Italian, they annotate grammatical gender realisation with Masculine (M) and Feminine (F). They additionally introduce entity-level identifiers to track references to the same entity across sentences. They apply this scheme to the existing gENder-IT dataset, thereby producing a gold-standard resource annotated with both semantic and grammatical gender information. This annotated dataset is then used to systematically evaluate how current MT systems handle gender in English–Italian translation.
AI.result: Using the ConGA-annotated version of gENder-IT, the authors find that current MT systems show a systematic overuse of masculine forms in Italian translations, even when feminine or ambiguous genders would be more appropriate. They also observe that feminine gender realisation is inconsistent, suggesting that systems fail to maintain correct feminine agreement across different grammatical categories and across sentences for the same entity. These findings quantitatively demonstrate persistent gender bias and structural weaknesses in existing MT systems’ handling of gender.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that current MT systems are not reliably gender-accurate when translating from English into Italian, systematically favouring masculine realisations and mishandling feminine forms. The ConGA framework, combined with the annotated gENder-IT dataset, provides a fine-grained, linguistically sound methodology and benchmark for diagnosing such biases. This resource can guide the development and evaluation of more gender-aware and robust multilingual NLP and MT systems, encouraging future work on better modeling of both semantic and grammatical gender across languages.
